title: Leather paring for turn-ins and hinges
subtitle: Thinning leather at the edges and corners before covering a book
excerpt: Full-thickness leather does not fold cleanly around board corners or into spine hinges. Paring thins the leather at every turn-in edge and joint so the covering lies flush with no lumps. Learning to pare accurately is the single skill that separates a clean leather binding from a stiff or lumpy one.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), public domain, chapter on covering and leather preparation. Cockerell gives detailed instructions for paring turn-ins, spine joints, and corner mitres.

glossaryTerms.definition: Shaving the flesh side of leather to reduce its thickness at edges and folds. Pared leather turns in around board edges without creating a visible ridge.
The strip of covering material that folds over the board edge and is glued to the inside face. In leather covering, the turn-ins must be pared thin enough to fold without stiffening the hinge.

content.text: Vegetable-tanned bookbinding leather at 1.0-1.5 mm is flexible enough to cover boards without

: the 15-20 mm strips that fold over the board edges: will create a hard ridge at each edge if left at full thickness. The spine joint demands even thinner leather because it must flex hundreds of times over the book's life. Paring solves both problems by gradually reducing the thickness from full to near-zero at the very edge.

attrs.heading: Safety: paring knife
attrs.body: The skiving knife used for paring is one of the sharpest tools in bookbinding. Always pare away from the body. Keep the leather flat on the cutting mat; never hold it in the air while paring. Change blades before any session: a dull blade skids.

items.name: Veg-tan leather, 1.0-1.5 mm (slug: veg-tan-leather-thin)
items.description: Cut the covering piece 30 mm larger than the board on all four sides to allow for turn-ins.

items.name: Craft knife with fresh No. 11 blades (slug: craft-knife)
items.description: For paring thin leather. A dedicated skiving knife is sharper but a fresh craft blade works for occasional use.

items.name: Self-healing cutting mat, A3 (slug: cutting-mat-a3)
items.description: Hard cutting mat; leather must be flat during paring.

items.name: Metal ruler, 30 cm (slug: metal-ruler-30cm)
items.description: For the straight-edge guide when thinning long turn-in strips.

content.text: Mark the turn-in width on the flesh side of the leather with a ballpoint: 18 mm in from each edge. The strip between the mark and the edge is the paring zone. Hold the craft knife at a very shallow angle, almost flat against the surface (about 10-15° from horizontal). Work from the mark toward the edge, shaving a thin consistent layer of flesh-side fibre. The target thickness at the outer edge is approximately 0.3 mm: thin enough to be slightly translucent when held to light.

The thinning is gradual, not sudden: from full thickness at the mark, progressively thinner toward the edge. Four or five passes usually reaches the correct taper on 1.0 mm leather; 1.5 mm leather needs more passes. Work in the same direction on each pass; returning the blade against the grain tears the fibres.

Corners must be thinned more aggressively than straight edges because a mitre fold at a corner stacks three layers of leather inside the turn-in. Mark a diagonal line across each corner from one turn-in mark to the other. Pare the corner triangle to near-paper thickness. When the leather folds around the corner, the mitre should sit flat against the board edge with no visible bump.

Before applying PVA, fold the turn-in over the edge of a scrap board and press it down with a bone folder. The leather should turn cleanly with no cracking or ridging at the fold line. If the fold shows a hard crease, pare thinner. If the leather tears at the fold, the paring was too aggressive at that point; patch with a thin sliver of new leather bonded with PVA and pare again.
